hook: A Byzantine cistern hidden beneath a working carpet shop a stone's throw south of the Hippodrome.
facts: An open subterranean Byzantine cistern lying under Nakkaş Oriental Rugs on Nakilbent Street, about 100 m south of the Hippodrome's sphendone in Sultanahmet.
The Byzantine Legacy dates it perhaps to the 5th century, while popular guides attribute it to the reign of Justinian (527-565); it measured roughly 9 x 34 m.

It was originally built with two rows of 9 columns (18 total); in the Ottoman period seven were replaced with reinforced brick piers, leaving nine columns with their original Corinthian capitals and imposts.
Scholars suggest it may have belonged to the Great Palace of Constantinople or to a building serving the Hippodrome.
It is restored and open to the public free of charge, and hosts a Hippodrome reconstruction exhibition (based on the 2010 Pera Museum show) and art events.
w: A perfectly preserved 6th-century Byzantine water tank, hidden in the basement under a Sultanahmet carpet shop. Brick vaults rise on old columns just south of the Hippodrome, restored and free to step into -- and sometimes hung as an intimate art space. One of dozens of cisterns honeycombing the old city, but quieter than the famous ones.

hook: An aristocratic Byzantine family tomb buried in the Theodosian walls, its carved sarcophagi found by chance in 1988.
facts: A Byzantine hypogeum (underground tomb) discovered in 1988 north of the Gate of Pege (Silivrikapı), between the inner and outer Theodosian Walls at the base of Tower 37.

The chamber measures about 7.70 x 6.30 m, has a north-south orientation and a gable roof of limestone slabs, comprising a burial chamber and an antechamber; it is dated to roughly the early 5th century.

It held five sarcophagi (one marble, four limestone) carved with Christian reliefs including Christ and the Twelve Apostles, Moses receiving the Law and the Sacrifice of Isaac; the marble coffin bore a christogram flanked by candlesticks.
Believed to be the tomb of an aristocratic family, with relief styles dated to the late 4th-early 5th century.

Originals damaged by treasure-hunters were moved to the Istanbul Archaeological Museums, with copies left in place; a city-wall visitor centre opened at Silivrikapı in September 2022 incorporating the hypogeum.
w: A rare 5th-century Byzantine family tomb, tucked into the Theodosian walls by the Silivri Gate and found only in 1988. Its walls carry early-Christian sarcophagus reliefs -- Christ, the apostles, scenes from scripture -- carved when the empire was still young in its faith. A nobleman's burial vault, sealed in the city ramparts.

hook: The 6th-century church of SS Sergius and Bacchus, a domed dry-run for Hagia Sophia, still ringed by Justinian's inscription.
facts: Formerly the Church of Saints Sergius and Bacchus, built between 527 and 536 AD under Emperor Justinian I as one of the first acts of his reign.

Its two-storey colonnade carries an inscription of twelve Greek hexameters dedicated to Justinian, his wife Theodora and Saint Sergius; many column capitals still bear the monograms of Justinian and Theodora.
The columns are of verd antique and red Synnadic marble - 16 in the lower storey, 18 in the upper.
It was converted into the Küçük Ayasofya mosque between 1506 and 1513 by Hüseyin Ağa, chief eunuch (kapı ağası) of Bayezid II's court.
It functions today as an active mosque, open to visitors with no entrance fee outside prayer times.
w: The dry run for Hagia Sophia -- the church of Saints Sergius and Bacchus, built by Justinian in the 530s a few years before its colossal namesake, and a likely testing ground for its dome. An undulating columned gallery rings the interior, its carved frieze still naming the imperial couple. A mosque now, off the Kumkapi backstreets.

n: Church of St Mary of the Mongols
hook: The one Byzantine church of Constantinople that has never stopped being Greek Orthodox - locals call it the Bloody Church.
facts: Known in Greek as Theotokos Panagiotissa / Panagia Mouchliotissa and in Turkish as Kanlı Kilise ('Bloody Church'); the sign above the door reads 'Meryem Ana Rum Ortodoks Kilisesi'.

It is the only surviving Byzantine church in Constantinople to have always remained a functioning Greek Orthodox house of worship - it and Hagia Irene are the only two Byzantine churches there never converted into a mosque.

Tradition holds the church was founded in the 7th century by a daughter of Emperor Maurice; in 1281 Maria Palaiologina, illegitimate daughter of Michael VIII and widow of Abaqa Khan of the Mongols, rebuilt it and retired there, giving it its name.
Under Selim I and Ahmed II there were Ottoman attempts to convert it, but grants by Mehmed II and Bayezid II kept it a Greek parish.
It sits at the top of a slope in Fener overlooking the Golden Horn, behind a high wall, and is usually not open to the public.
legend: Tradition holds that Mehmed II endowed the church to the mother of Christodoulos, the Greek architect of the Fatih Mosque, in gratitude for his work.
w: The one Byzantine church in Istanbul never turned into a mosque -- still Greek Orthodox after five centuries, up a Fener backstreet. A Mongol khan's widow, a Byzantine princess, refounded it on her return home; a sultan's decree spared it at the Conquest. Locals call it the Bloody Church. Legend even gives it a tunnel to Hagia Sophia.

hook: Three Byzantine churches fused into one mosque - the grandest Komnenian complex left in the city after Hagia Sophia.
facts: Built between 1118 and 1136 under Emperor John II Komnenos and Empress Irene of Hungary as the Monastery of Christ Pantokrator, joining three interconnected churches plus a chapel; it is regarded as the best example of Middle Byzantine architecture in Constantinople.
The original complex also held a hospital, hospice, library and cemetery, making it one of the largest Byzantine religious foundations after Hagia Sophia.
After the 1453 conquest the main church was converted to a mosque and the monastery served as a medrese; the Ottomans named it after the scholar Molla Zeyrek, who taught there.
It has been part of the UNESCO World Heritage Site 'Historic Areas of Istanbul' since 1985, and was placed on the World Monuments Watch list of endangered monuments in 2004.
Following a long restoration, the mosque reopened to visitors on 29 May 2019.
w: The second-largest Byzantine structure left in the city after Hagia Sophia -- three linked churches of the 12th-century Pantokrator Monastery, once an imperial mausoleum and a great hospital. It crowns a crumbling quarter of leaning wooden Ottoman houses above the Golden Horn. UNESCO-listed, restored and reopened.

hook: The supreme survival of late-Byzantine art - walls and domes carpeted with 14th-century mosaics and frescoes.
facts: The former Church of the Holy Saviour in Chora, near the Edirnekapı section of the Theodosian Walls, holds some of the finest surviving mosaics and frescoes of the Late Byzantine period, depicting the lives of Christ and the Virgin.
Its mosaic and fresco programme - including the Christ Pantocrator, the Virgin and Child, the life of the Virgin and Gospel scenes - was created in the early 14th century.
It was converted from church to mosque in the Ottoman era and later served as the Kariye Museum.
It reopened for regular Muslim worship as Kariye Mosque on 6 May 2024; its mosaics and frescoes remain viewable outside prayer times, and it is closed to tourist visits on Fridays.
w: A small late-Byzantine church near the walls, lined with the most dazzling mosaics and frescoes in the city -- gold-ground scenes of Christ and the Last Judgment, made around 1320. Long a museum, it reverted to a mosque in 2024; the art remains, draped at prayer times. The most beautiful hidden interior in Istanbul, in a quiet corner.

hook: Iznik-tiled octagonal mausoleum of a Byzantine-born grand vizier executed by Mehmed II in 1474, tucked in the bazaar backstreets.
facts: Octagonal tomb dated by an entrance inscription to AH 878 (1473-74 CE), part of the Mahmut Pasha mosque complex begun in 1462.
Its exterior is faced with turquoise and indigo tiles forming geometric patterns, a treatment described as unique among Ottoman mausolea in Istanbul.
Holds Veli Mahmud Pasha, a grand vizier of Byzantine/Greek origin who converted to Islam and served Sultan Mehmed II.
Mahmud Pasha fell from favour and was executed on the sultan's order in 1474; the tomb was completed around the time of his death.
The surrounding Mahmutpaşa bazaar quarter, including the mosque, hamam and fountains, takes its name from him.
w: The oldest tiled mausoleum in Istanbul, its facade set with rare early Iznik-style tiles in blue and turquoise -- the tomb of Mahmud Pasha, a Serbian-born grand vizier strangled on the sultan's order in 1474. His fall is wrapped in court intrigue and personal scandal. A jewel-box of a grave, behind the Grand Bazaar.

hook: Sinan's tile-drenched jewel-box mosque, raised on a terrace above the spice-market shops and clad in the finest Iznik tiles in the city.
facts: Designed by the imperial architect Mimar Sinan and completed around 1561-1563 for grand vizier Rüstem Pasha, son-in-law of Süleyman I.
Almost every vertical surface inside and under the entrance porch is covered in Iznik tiles, roughly 2,300 tiles in about 80 patterns, the most lavish tilework of any Ottoman mosque.
Built on a high terrace over a complex of vaulted shops whose rents endowed the mosque, raising it above the market streets.
Stands in the Hasırcılar Çarşısı (Strawmat Weavers' Market) in the Tahtakale neighbourhood near the Spice Bazaar.
Its octagonal-baldachin plan, with the dome carried on eight piers, was the first time Sinan used this scheme.
w: A small Sinan mosque raised above the Spice Bazaar's shops, sheathed almost wholly in the finest Iznik tiles ever made -- thousands of them, flowering across every wall in tomato-red and blue. Built around 1563 for Suleyman's powerful, much-hated son-in-law. You climb a hidden stair to find it. A tiled jewel, floating over the market din.

hook: A Roman aqueduct of double-tiered arches striding 29 m over a six-lane boulevard, still threading the heart of the old city after 1,650 years.
facts: Completed in 373 CE under the emperor Valens, carrying water across the valley between the third and fourth hills of Constantinople.
The surviving span is about 921 m long (originally roughly 971 m) and reaches a maximum height of around 29 m, with a total of 86 arches.
Different arch sections date to different eras: arches 1-40 and 46-51 to Valens, 41-45 restored by Mustafa II, and 47-51 rebuilt (in ogival form) by Süleyman the Magnificent.
It fed Constantinople's water system, including the great cisterns, and was repaired across the centuries by emperors such as Constantine V and Basil II.
Its arches today span Atatürk Boulevard between Istanbul University and the Fatih Mosque district.
w: A great Roman aqueduct of double arches striding clean across the city, finished around 373 under the emperor Valens -- a kilometre of it still standing, a busy road now running beneath its spans. It carried water to the heart of Constantinople for a thousand years and more. Highway traffic, threading the arches of a vanished empire.

n: Edirnekapı Surları (Theodosian Walls at the Charisius Gate)
hook: The highest, most atmospheric stretch of the 5th-century land walls, at the gate where Mehmed II rode into the conquered city in 1453.
facts: Edirnekapı, the ancient Gate of Charisius, sits on the sixth hill, the highest point of the walled city at about 77 m, where the Theodosian land walls reach their loftiest run.
The triple Theodosian land walls were completed in the early 5th century under Theodosius II and withstood sieges for a thousand years.
After breaching the walls near the Charisius and St Romanus gates, Mehmed II made his ceremonial entry into Constantinople through this gate in 1453.
The gate was later restored under Bayezid II and used as a ceremonial entrance by Ottoman sultans; an inscription records wall repairs here.
The stretch lies just north of the Kariye (Chora) church-mosque, making it a defensible, walkable section distinct from the Yedikule fortress to the south.
w: The highest, fiercest stretch of the great Theodosian land walls -- triple lines of fifth-century brick and stone that held off the world until 1453. Here at the Charisius Gate, the highest point, the last emperor is said to have fallen as the city finally broke. You can climb the towers and walk the gritty ramparts.

hook: Istanbul's largest 17th-century caravanserai, hiding a Byzantine watchtower in its corner and one of the city's great clandestine rooftop views.
facts: Founded in 1651 by Kösem Valide Sultan, the powerful mother of sultans Murad IV and Ibrahim, as the city's largest han.
Its northeastern corner incorporates a large Byzantine stone tower (the Tower of Eirene), said to have originally stood about 27 m and the only such tower still standing in the city.
Roughly three courtyards deep, it long housed traders and is now full of workshops and offices, with a small Shi'a mosque serving the Iranian community.
Its rooftop offers a sweeping panorama of the Golden Horn, Bosphorus, old city and Galata.
The han's rooftops appeared in the 2012 James Bond film Skyfall.
legend: Local lore holds that Kösem Sultan used the old Byzantine corner tower as a private treasury for her wealth.
w: A vast 17th-century caravanserai near the Golden Horn, built by the formidable Kosem Sultan -- a labyrinth of courtyards and workshops with a ruined Byzantine tower swallowed inside it, said to hide treasure. Climb through the textile traders to a rooftop crowded with satellite dishes and one of the great hidden views of the old city.

hook: The first great Ottoman Baroque mosque, all scrolls and curves, its western gate opening straight onto the Grand Bazaar.
facts: Begun by Sultan Mahmud I in 1748 and completed under his successor Osman III in 1755.
It was the first monumental Ottoman building in the new Ottoman Baroque style, importing European baroque and neoclassical scrolls, shells and undulating cornices.
Among its architects was Simeon Kalfa, a Greek non-Muslim master, an unusual lead role for a major imperial Ottoman project.
Its prayer hall is lit by 174 windows, inspiring the name Nuruosmaniye, 'the Light of Osman'; the dome is about 25.4 m across, among the largest in the city.
The complex (mosque, madrasa, imaret, tomb, fountain and library) stands at the edge of the Grand Bazaar in Çemberlitaş, its western gate facing a bazaar entrance.
w: The first Ottoman baroque mosque, finished in 1755 at the gate of the Grand Bazaar -- all sweeping curves, a horseshoe courtyard and light flooding through ranks of windows, a sharp turn from the old Sinan style. Far fewer crowds reach it than its giant neighbours. Curves and light, where the empire started looking west.

hook: One of Istanbul's oldest synagogues, named for the Macedonian city of Ohrid and famed for a reading platform carved in the shape of a ship's prow.
facts: Founded around 1430 by Romaniote (Greek-speaking) Jews who had come from Ohrid (Greek 'Ahrid'), then in the Ottoman Empire and now in North Macedonia, making it one of the two oldest surviving synagogues in the Golden Horn area.
Its central tevah (reading platform, the Sephardic term for a bimah) is built in the shape of a boat's prow, an unusual form not found in most Istanbul synagogues.
It was rebuilt in the Ottoman Baroque style after a fire, following a sultan's decree in 1694, and has been restored repeatedly (recorded works in 1694, 1732, 1840, 1893, 1926 and 1955).
It was extensively renovated in 1992 by the Quincentennial Foundation to mark the 500th anniversary of the arrival of Sephardic Jews expelled from Spain into the Ottoman Empire.
It stands in Ayvansaray/Balat, the historic Jewish quarter of the Fatih district on the Golden Horn.
legend: The boat-shaped tevah is popularly said to represent either Noah's Ark or the ships that carried Sephardic Jews from Iberia to the Ottoman Empire in 1492.
w: One of the oldest synagogues in Istanbul, in continuous use in Balat since around 1430 -- famous for its tevah, the reading platform, carved in the shape of a ship's prow, read as Noah's Ark or the boats that carried Sephardic exiles here. Behind a plain locked gate, by appointment. A boat in wood, in the old Jewish quarter.

hook: A red-brick neo-Gothic 'castle' looming over Fener, the oldest surviving Greek school in Istanbul and a landmark of the city's dwindling Greek Orthodox community.
facts: Known in Turkish as Fener Rum Lisesi and in Greek as the 'Great School of the Nation' (Megali tou Genous Scholi), it traces its institutional origins to 1454, founded under Patriarch Gennadios Scholarios shortly after the Ottoman conquest.
The present landmark building was erected between 1881 and 1883 to the design of the Ottoman Greek architect Konstantinos Dimadis.
Built of red brick in an eclectic style evoking European chateaux and medieval castles, it rises about 40 metres over six levels, earning the nicknames 'Red Castle' and 'Red School'.
Historically it educated Phanariot Greek elites; alumni include the scholar and Moldavian prince Dimitrie Cantemir.

It stands in the Fener neighbourhood near the Church of St George, seat of the Ecumenical Patriarchate, and remains in operation though with very few students reflecting the small remaining Greek population.
w: A great red-brick castle of a school looming over Fener, built 1881-83 with a domed astronomical observatory on top. The Phanar college taught the Ottoman empire's Greek elite, princes and dragomans among them, for centuries; now a few dozen pupils rattle around it. The Red Castle, half-empty above the Golden Horn.

hook: Miniature stone 'palaces' for sparrows and swallows built into the walls of an 18th-century Üsküdar mosque, one of the best-preserved sets of Ottoman birdhouses (kuş köşkü).
facts: The Yeni Valide Mosque (Yeni Valide Camii) was built between 1708 and 1710 by order of Sultan Ahmed III for his mother, Emetullah Gülnuş Sultan, in the centre of Üsküdar on the Asian shore.
Birdhouses survive on three facades of the mosque complex and are considered some of the best-preserved examples of the Ottoman 'bird palace' (kuş sarayı / kuş köşkü) tradition.
Of the surviving birdhouses, one is shaped like a house and two are shaped like miniature mosques complete with twin minarets.
The most elaborate examples date from techniques used at the end of the 18th century, combining lattice windows, crenellations, cupolas and minarets at a doll's-house scale.

Such birdhouses served to shelter birds and to keep droppings off the masonry; building one was regarded as a charitable act earning religious merit, reflecting an Ottoman ethic of compassion toward animals.
w: On the qibla wall of Uskudar's Yeni Valide Mosque cling tiny stone bird palaces -- ornate little birdhouses with domes and balconies, set into the masonry so sparrows could nest and their song rise with the prayers. The Ottomans called them kus kosku, bird kiosks, an everyday kindness in stone. Compassion, carved for the sparrows.

hook: A serpentine flight of steps in Galata whose interlocking curving runs of stone form one of Istanbul's earliest pieces of Art-Nouveau urban design.
facts: The Camondo Stairs (Turkish: Kamondo Merdivenleri) are a 19th-century stairway off Bankalar Caddesi (Banks Street) in the Galata quarter, modern Karaköy, in the Beyoğlu district of Istanbul.
They were designed in a distinctive blend of Neo-Baroque and early Art-Nouveau styles, with two curving runs of steps that split and rejoin so the descending and ascending paths interlace.

They were commissioned and built circa 1870-1880 by Abraham Salomon Camondo, the Ottoman-Venetian Jewish banker and patriarch of the House of Camondo, a financier dynasty often likened to the Rothschilds.

Camondo reportedly had them built to give his family an easier connection between his residence on Kart Çınar Sokak and his banking offices on Bankalar Caddesi, and so his children could reach the nearby Austrian high school.
The stairs were famously photographed by Henri Cartier-Bresson in 1964, an image that became an iconic representation of Istanbul's architectural heritage.
w: A serpentine flight of steps spilling down a Galata hill in interlocking curves -- a flourish of early Art Nouveau built around 1870 by Abraham Salomon Camondo, head of a great Jewish banking dynasty. Cartier-Bresson photographed it in 1964 and made it famous. A banker's staircase, frozen mid-swirl.

hook: A narrow, multi-floor arcade off İstiklal Avenue crammed with second-hand booksellers, a labyrinthine paradise of old print, comics, posters and vinyl.
facts: Aslıhan Pasajı is a covered arcade reached from a side street directly across from Galatasaray High School (Galatasaray Lisesi) on İstiklal Avenue, in the Beyoğlu district.
It is one of the best-known passages of the Beyoğlu Sahaflar Çarşısı (second-hand booksellers' bazaar), a long, narrow, multi-storey warren of shops devoted to used books.
Its dozens of sahaf (second-hand bookseller) stalls deal in old and rare books across many languages and genres, as well as comics, magazines, posters and vinyl records.
It has served as an intellectual refuge in Beyoğlu since the 1960s, and during the 1980s and 1990s its booksellers quietly carried hard-to-find political and dissident literature.
w: A hidden multi-floor warren of second-hand booksellers off Istiklal, by Galatasaray -- the old sahaf bazaar of Beyoglu, stalls packed floor to ceiling with worn books, faded magazines, comics, posters and crates of vinyl. You burrow rather than browse. A paper labyrinth, behind the avenue's noise.

hook: Asian-shore Bosphorus village where churches, synagogues and a mosque share one short street of painted wooden houses.
facts: Kuzguncuk is a neighbourhood of Üsküdar district on the Asian side of the Bosphorus, between Üsküdar and Beylerbeyi; its name means 'little raven'.
İcadiye Caddesi is its lively main street, under 1 km long, lined with 19th- and early-20th-century balconied wooden houses, cafes, antique and boutique shops.
Documented Jewish settlement dates to the 16th century (earliest record 1562); Armenians arrived in the 18th century, and it long held Greek, Armenian and Jewish communities together.
The neighbourhood contains two synagogues (Bet Yaakov, 1878; Bet Nissim, 1840s), four churches and two mosques, several clustered near the main street.
Its preserved Ottoman wooden houses have made it a frequent filming location for Turkish television since the mid-1980s.
w: A village that time forgot on the Asian shore -- one low street of painted wooden houses where a mosque, a church and two synagogues stand almost shoulder to shoulder, a survival of old Istanbul's mixed Greek, Jewish, Armenian and Turkish life. A market garden still grows behind the houses. Peaceful, photogenic, and quietly multicultural.

hook: Rainbow row of pastel Ottoman houses climbing a Golden Horn hillside in Istanbul's old Jewish quarter.
facts: Balat, on the Golden Horn in the Fatih district, is one of Istanbul's oldest neighbourhoods, historically the city's main Jewish quarter after Sephardic Jews were welcomed following their 1492 expulsion from Spain.
Kiremit Caddesi is the most-photographed street, lined with painted historic wooden houses, some around two centuries old.
On the adjacent Merdivenli Yokuş, a stepped lane, sits a famous row of pastel houses originally built for well-off Greek residents.
Over centuries Balat mixed Jewish, Greek Orthodox, Armenian and Muslim communities.
The colourful-houses streets have become one of Istanbul's most popular social-media photo destinations.
legend: A local saying holds that the house colours signalled communities - blue for Jewish, green for Greek and red for Armenian residents - though this is folklore rather than documented planning.
w: A steep row of rainbow-painted houses tumbling down a Balat hill, the most photographed corner of the old Jewish and Greek quarter on the Golden Horn. Once poor and forgotten, the district turned hip -- cafes and street art -- but the layered history shows under the new paint. Instagram's Istanbul, with five centuries behind it.

hook: The spiritual seat of world Orthodoxy, hidden behind walls in the old Greek lighthouse quarter on the Golden Horn.
facts: The Church of St George in the Fener (Phanar) district has been the headquarters of the Ecumenical Patriarchate of Constantinople since 1586.
Its address is Dr Sadık Ahmet Caddesi No 19, Fener, in the Fatih district on the Golden Horn.
The Ecumenical Patriarch is regarded as 'first among equals' and spiritual leader of the world's Eastern Orthodox Christians.
'Phanar' (Fener) means lantern or lighthouse in Greek, after the old lighthouse quarter that became the main Greek district under the Ottomans.
The Fener neighbourhood is distinct from neighbouring Balat, sitting just to its east along the Golden Horn shore.
w: The spiritual centre of the world's Orthodox Christians, tucked behind walls in Fener -- the seat of the Ecumenical Patriarch of Constantinople, first among equals since Byzantium. Its modest church of St George holds relics and an old patriarchal throne. The main gate has stayed welded shut since a patriarch was hanged from it in 1821.

hook: A hollow of cobbled Beyoğlu lanes packed with antique dealers, just downhill from İstiklal.
facts: Çukurcuma is an antique-hunting quarter in Beyoğlu, officially within Firuzağa Mahallesi, a short walk downhill from İstiklal Avenue and Galatasaray.
The name combines 'çukur' (hollow) and 'cuma' (Friday); the district hosts well over a hundred antique and vintage shops.
Its streets retain restored 19th-century buildings, wooden houses and cobblestone alleys from the Ottoman era.
The Ömer Ağa Fountain on Çukurcuma Caddesi dates to 1731, near the Muhyiddin Molla Fenari Mosque.
Shops are run largely by minority and Levantine families long resident in the area, selling Ottoman furniture, carpets and bric-a-brac.
w: A knot of steep Beyoglu lanes packed with antique and junk shops -- floor-to-ceiling chandeliers, gramophones, old photographs, faded furniture, the salvage of a century of Istanbul households. It is slow-wander territory, made for getting lost and turning something up. Orhan Pamuk set his Museum of Innocence among these very streets.

hook: A waterline row of timber Ottoman yalı mansions, oriels almost touching the Bosphorus, between Bebek and Kuruçeşme.
facts: Arnavutköy, on the European shore between Bebek and Kuruçeşme, is famous for its row of wooden yalı - multi-storey timber mansions lining the Bosphorus waterfront, many with Art Nouveau detailing.
The name means 'Albanian village', linked to Albanian workers brought to Istanbul to pave the city's streets under Sultan Abdülmecid in the 19th century.
The neighbourhood's population was historically a mix of Armenian, Greek and Jewish residents.
The oldest surviving yalı in the area, a large red mansion, is dated to roughly 1820-1830.
A ferry pier (Arnavutköy İskelesi) on the Eminönü-Sarıyer Bosphorus route sits at the centre of the waterfront.
w: A rare unbroken row of grand wooden yali -- Ottoman waterfront mansions -- on the Bosphorus shore at Arnavutkoy, bay windows leaning over the strait. The village kept its fishermen's lanes and pastel timber houses where others lost theirs. The best run of old yali left in the city. Wooden palaces, with their feet in the water.

hook: A 1720s Ottoman masonry dam deep in the Belgrad Forest, once a lifeline of Istanbul's water supply.
facts: Büyük Bent (the Great Dam) is an Ottoman reservoir dam in the Belgrad Forest on the European side, reachable from the town of Bahçeköy in Sarıyer.
The present dam was built in 1723-1724 under Sultan Ahmed III, feeding the Kırkçeşme water-supply system that brought water to Istanbul.
It is one of seven main historic dams/bents in the forest, built or rebuilt between roughly 1620 and 1839.
Belgrad Forest itself covers about 5,500 hectares of mixed woodland and is the city's largest near-urban forest.
The Büyük Bent area is a popular picnic and walking destination among the forest's seven main recreation grounds.
w: A great Ottoman dam deep in the Belgrad Forest, the cool woodland the city long fled to in summer -- the Buyuk Bent, a stone reservoir of the 1720s that fed Istanbul's fountains through old aqueducts. Walkers and runners loop the wooded trails past a string of these historic bents. The waterworks of an empire, gone to forest.

hook: A hillside Bosphorus park of Ottoman pavilions that erupts in tulips every April.
facts: Emirgan Park (Emirgan Korusu) is a public park on the European Bosphorus shore in the Sarıyer district, covering about 470,000 m² (117 acres).
It hosts the annual Istanbul Tulip Festival each April, held since 2005 and evoking the Ottoman 'Tulip Period' of 1718-1730.
Three 19th-century wooden pavilions built by Khedive Ismail Pasha - the Yellow, Pink and White Köşk - now serve as cafes and restaurants; the Yellow Köşk (1871-1878) was a hunting lodge.
The estate passed from the deposed Egyptian Khedive's heirs to private owners in the 1930s before being donated to Istanbul's municipality in the 1940s.
The park holds more than 120 plant species and two ornamental ponds, and is managed by the Istanbul Metropolitan Municipality.
w: A lush historic park climbing back from the Bosphorus, scattered with gilded Ottoman pavilions -- a yellow, a pink and a white kosk -- and famous for its spring tulips, blazing each April. The old Khedive of Egypt's estate, now a public garden. Tea in a pavilion, the strait glinting below. A genuine green secret on the shore.

hook: A ruined Byzantine-Genoese fortress on the hill where the Bosphorus meets the Black Sea, above the last ferry village.
facts: Yoros Castle stands on a hilltop above Anadolu Kavağı, a fishing village at the Asian mouth of the Bosphorus in the Beykoz district, about 110 m above sea level.
It is a 13th-century Byzantine fortress, generally attributed to Manuel I Komnenos or Michael VIII Palaiologos, later reinforced by the Genoese in the 15th century.
Sultan Bayezid I captured it in 1391 and used it as a base while building Anadolu Hisarı for his blockade of Constantinople.
After 1453 the Ottomans drove out the Genoese, who held a strategically threatening position near the new capital.
Anadolu Kavağı is the northern terminus of the long Bosphorus tourist ferry from Eminönü.
legend: It is said a great chain could be stretched from Yoros to a fortress on the opposite Bosphorus shore to bar enemy warships, echoing the chain once strung across the Golden Horn - a claim repeated in popular histories but not firmly documented.
w: A ruined Byzantine castle on a hill where the Bosphorus meets the Black Sea, above the sleepy fishing village of Anadolu Kavagi at the end of the ferry line. Genoese and Ottomans fought over its towers; a great chain once closed the strait below. Climb up for the ruins and the view, then drop down for fresh fish on the quay.
